food_subgroup: Pork dishes — cutlet / chop, pan-fried or braised

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311349 represents pork cutlet as a food product category; used as closest parent as no Malaysia-specific pork cutlet entry exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers pig meat cuts broadly; no specific pork cutlet prepared dish code exists in FoodEx2; used as closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork cutlet in a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker or restaurant context is approximately 150 g, comprising one or two pieces of pork loin or shoulder, pan-fried or braised, served with rice or soup.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork loin (braised, lean and fat) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values are adapted to reflect a pan-fried or braised pork cutlet preparation typical of Malaysian and Singaporean hawker contexts. Cooking oil absorption and soy-sauce marinade contribute marginally to fat and sodium; sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce seasoning. Core macros are directly from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork loin data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — meat and protein foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure meat and protein foods have a negligible glycemic index because they contain virtually no digestible carbohydrate. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables consistently classify meat products as GI < 10 (effectively zero). Pork cutlet contains approximately 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g (from marinade and coating), yielding 1.8 g carbohydrate per 150 g serving. Per Veritas v2 convention for foods with effectively zero GI, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is 'low'. The glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as (1.8 g carbohydrate * 1) / 100 = 0.018, rounded to 0.0. Analogous estimate based on meat category classification in University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving of pork cutlet prepared with soy-sauce marinade contributes approximately 630 mg sodium, representing about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by health authorities; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pork cutlet contains approximately 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, producing a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or sugary sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 150 g serving provides approximately 7.2 g saturated fat and 132 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean cuts, limit portion size, and prefer braising over deep-frying to reduce total fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork cutlet (猪排, Zhu Pai) is a popular meat dish served across Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and casual restaurants. It consists of a slice of pork loin or shoulder that is marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, garlic, five-spice powder, and sometimes egg and cornstarch, then pan-fried or braised until cooked through with a lightly caramelised exterior. The dish is characterised by a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile derived from the soy-sauce marinade and the Maillard browning of the pork surface during frying. The texture is firm yet juicy when prepared from loin cuts, or slightly more tender when shoulder cuts are used and braised. In the context of Bak Kut Teh restaurants such as Legendary Bak Kut Teh, pork cutlet may be served as a side dish alongside the signature pork rib soup, accompanied by steamed white rice, braised tofu, and preserved vegetables. Nutritionally, pork cutlet is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 27.5 g protein and 13.8 g fat per 100 g, with negligible carbohydrate content (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g). It is a good source of B-vitamins including thiamine (B1), niacin (B3), and B6, as well as zinc, selenium, and phosphorus. The primary clinical considerations are its moderate sodium content from soy-sauce seasoning (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) and its saturated fat content (approximately 4.8 g per 100 g), which are relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic impact is negligible, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and high-carbohydrate accompaniments are limited.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork cutlet?
Is pork cutlet high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork loin data, which is a well-validated primary source for pork macronutrients. Adaptation was required because no exact Malaysian or Singaporean pork cutlet entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the time of processing. Sodium was adjusted upward to reflect typical soy-sauce marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate based on the meat category classification in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked pork loin, braised, lean and fat, FNDDS NDB 10219 / FDC ID 168323). No exact pork cutlet entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD; USDA SR Legacy is used as the highest-priority available approved source for this food. 2. Sodium_mg is adjusted to approximately 420 mg per 100 g to reflect typical soy-sauce marinade contribution in Malaysian/Singaporean preparation; the USDA base value for unseasoned braised pork loin is approximately 65 mg per 100 g. 3. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 for pure meat; this is confirmed zero as pork muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. 4. GI is set to 1 (effectively zero) as pork cutlet is a protein-dominant food with negligible digestible carbohydrate; gi_category is 'low'. The glycemic_load_per_serving of 0.0 is calculated as (1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 150 g serving / 100) × GI_fraction (1), rounded to one decimal place. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168323 and are reported as numeric values; none are -1 as the USDA source reports these nutrients. 6. The food_name in the input JSON was 'B9 Pork Cutlet 猪排'; 'B9' is a menu item identifier from Legendary Bak Kut Teh and is not part of the canonical food name; it has been preserved in aliases as 'B9 Pork Cutlet'. 7. Cuisine is classified as Malaysian per the input record and the serving restaurant context; the dish is also common in Singaporean hawker culture. Multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine multilingual requirement. 8. SNOMED CT code 226803007 'Pork cutlet (food)' is used as an exact match. FoodOn FOODON_03311349 and FoodEx2 A0CKW are used as closest parent matches. 9. glycemic_index field is set to 1 to represent effectively zero GI for this protein-dominant food; gi_category is 'low'. The glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed menu identifier 'B9' from canonical food_name; preserved as alias 'B9 Pork Cutlet'.
Added Chinese traditional form '豬排' in addition to simplified '猪排'.
Added Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names per Singaporean/Malaysian multilingual requirement.
Adapted USDA SR Legacy cooked pork loin nutrient values; adjusted sodium upward to reflect soy-sauce marinade.
Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 and glycemic_load_per_serving from 0.2 to 0.0, per Veritas v2 rule for effectively zero GI foods, and updated all related notes and justifications.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are fully consistent.
Documented dietary_fibre_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero for pure meat tissue.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'exact' and 'closest_parent'.
Ensured all per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values used as USDA source reports all listed nutrients.
Provided specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
